coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescent probe based on N, B, F co-doped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive determination of sulfathiazole
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Letian
Liu, Yongli
Cheng, Guohao
Fan, Zegang
Yuan, Jinyu
He, Shuailong
Zhu, Guifen
coredata.dc:description: The widespread occurrence of sulfathiazole (STZ) in the environment has raised concerns regarding the potential risks to ecosystem and human health. Thus, there is a need to develop facile and efficient methods for monitoring STZ. In this study, a novel fluorescent probe, based on N, B, F co-doped carbon dots (N, B, F-CDs), was developed for the highly sensitive and selective determination of STZ. The fluorescent N, B, F-CDs were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method using malonate and 1-allyl-3-vinylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ionic liquid as precursors. The obtained N, B, F-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence response toward STZ due to the inner filter effect (IFE), which caused the fluorescence to be quenched. The fluorescent probe allowed the STZ concentration to be accurately determined with a low detection limit of 5.5 ng mL−1 in two wide linear ranges of 0.008–10 μg mL−1 and 10–45 μg mL−1. The practicability of the fluorescent probe was further validated in river water, soil, milk, and egg samples, and the satisfactory spiked recoveries of STZ ranged from 96.1 to 101.6%. The proposed fluorescent probe based on N, B, F-CDs can be easily prepared and possess high selectivity and sensitivity, thereby displaying its tremendous potential for the identification and determination of STZ in the environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N, B, F co-doped carbon dots
Fluorescence probe
Sulfathiazole
1-Allyl-3vinylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of STZ and interference experiments
Optical properties of N, B, F-CDs
Mechanism for the detection of STZ
Optimization of N, B, F-CDs concentration
Sensitivity of N, B, F-CDs probe for STZ detection
Analysis of STZ in actual samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of synthesis and detection principle of fluorescent N, B, F-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of N, B, F-CDs in absence and presence of STZ (10 μg mL−1). (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of N, B, F-CDs, STZ and N, B, F-CDs + STZ. (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum of STZ and maximum excitation and emission spectra of the N, B, F-CDs. (D) Fluorescence decay curves of prepared N, B, F-CDs (a) and N, B, F-CDs + STZ (b).

(A) Fluorescence responses of N, B, F-CDs on addition of different concentrations of STZ. (B) Plots of (F0 F)/F against the concentrations of STZ within the range of 0–50 μg mL−1. (C) and (D) The linear correlation of (F0 F)/F values to the concentration of STZ over the range from 0.008 to 10 μg mL−1 and 10 to 45 μg mL−1.

Selectivity of N, B, F-CDs obtained from individual of STZ with other analogues and mixture of STZ with other analogues (The concentrations of STZ and analogues: 0.1 μg mL−1 STZ, 0.5 μg mL−1 SPD, MD and SMO, 0.3 μg mL−1 SDZ, SMM and SMZ).
Comparison of different methods for STZ detection.
Spiked recoveries for STZ detected by the fluorescent probe (n = 6).

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescent probe based on N, B, F co-doped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive determination of sulfathiazole

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment, Henan Key Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control, Key Laboratory for Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental Pollution Control, Ministry of Education, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan 453007, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control
Key Laboratory for Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environment, Henan Key Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control, Key Laboratory for Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental Pollution Control, Ministry of Education, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan 453007, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The widespread occurrence of sulfathiazole (STZ) in the environment has raised concerns regarding the potential risks to ecosystem and human health. Thus, there is a need to develop facile and efficient methods for monitoring STZ. In this study, a novel fluorescent probe, based on N, B, F co-doped carbon dots (N, B, F-CDs), was developed for the highly sensitive and selective determination of STZ. The fluorescent N, B, F-CDs were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method using malonate and 1-allyl-3-vinylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ionic liquid as precursors. The obtained N, B, F-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence response toward STZ due to the inner filter effect (IFE), which caused the fluorescence to be quenched. The fluorescent probe allowed the STZ concentration to be accurately determined with a low detection limit of 5.5 ng mL−1 in two wide linear ranges of 0.008–10 μg mL−1 and 10–45 μg mL−1. The practicability of the fluorescent probe was further validated in river water, soil, milk, and egg samples, and the satisfactory spiked recoveries of STZ ranged from 96.1 to 101.6%. The proposed fluorescent probe based on N, B, F-CDs can be easily prepared and possess high selectivity and sensitivity, thereby displaying its tremendous potential for the identification and determination of STZ in the environment.

ce:keyword.ce:text: N, B, F co-doped carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfonamides are an important class of broad-spectrum antibiotics and their low-price and excellent antimicrobial activity has allowed them to be extensively used for the prevention and treatment of infections or diseases in the fields of medicine, animal husbandry, aquaculture etc. (Cui et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2019). However, the long-term overuse of these antibiotics causes more than 20,000 tons of sulfonamide antibiotics to be discharged into the environment each year, which results in the formation of sulfonamide residues (Dmitrienko et al., 2014). Sulfanilamide antibiotics possess high toxicity and stability, and even at ng L−1 concentration, their residues can severely affect the environment and the health of living organisms (Deng et al., 2019; Qiu et al., 2019; Yin et al., 2018). Sulfathiazole (STZ) is a common sulfonamide antibiotic, which has been found in numerous aquatic environments, soils, and sediments across the world (Liu et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2019). Due to its high toxicity and stability, the widespread occurrence of STZ in the environment has raised increasing concerns. Therefore, there is a need to explore facile and effective methods for the detection of trace amounts of STZ in the environment. Various techniques such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (Chen et al., 2016), electrochemical methods (Bueno et al., 2014), and solid-phase extraction coupled with liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (Bajkacz et al., 2020) have been reported for the detection of antibiotic residues. However, the wide-spread application of these methods is limited because they are expensive, time-consuming, and require the use of sophisticated processes. Thus, it is essential to propose a convenient and effective method for the rapid detection of STZ residues in the environment.

Carbon dots (CDs) are novel nanomaterials, which have a size of less than 10 nm. CDs have a wide range of applications in sensing, bioimaging, nanomedicine, catalysis, drug delivery, and optoelectronic devices because of their excellent properties, such as good biocompatibility, low toxicity, easy surface functionalization, water solubility, and environmental friendliness (Ashrafizadeh et al., 2020; Shu et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2019a; Yarur et al., 2019). So far, some CDs have been applied as fluorescent probes to detect metal ions, inorganic anions, and organic pollutants by the fluorescence quenching and recovery mechanisms (Han et al., 2019b; Liu et al., 2017b; Placido et al., 2019; Qian et al., 2019; Singh et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019b; Wang et al., 2020; Yarur et al., 2019). Li et al. discovered that highly luminescent N, S-CDs act as a superior ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of levofloxacin with a low detection limit of 5.1 μg L−1. The N, S-CDs were prepared via a one-step microwave-assisted method using L-cysteine and ammonium citrate as precursors (Li et al., 2018). In another study, Miao et al. employed CDs from tobacco to distinguish and detect three types of tetracyclines that exhibited different fluorescence—weak blue, navy-blue, and chartreuse—on a test strip. The detection limits for tetracycline, oxytetracycline, and chlorotetracycline were 5.18, 6.06, and 14 nmol L−1, respectively (Miao et al., 2018). These studies verify that CDs can be employed as fluorescent probes for the highly sensitive determination of trace organic pollutants in the environment.

CDs can be prepared from numerous precursors, such as carbon materials, small molecule and natural biomass (Dordevic et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2017a). These precursors are generally rich in nitrogen, sulfur, boron, fluorine, and other heteroatoms and, thus, can be used to prepare heteroatom doped CDs with unique photoluminescent properties and novel surface states (Liu et al., 2017a). Ionic liquids are low-temperature molten liquid salts composed of cations and anions. Most ionic liquids consist of nitrogen-containing cations and heteroatom-containing anions, making them ideal precursors for synthesizing CDs (Isosaari et al., 2019). In particular, imidazolium-based ionic liquids are beneficial for the synthesis of CDs owing to their aromatic structure and high nitrogen content (Liu et al., 2017a). Various studies have employed ionic liquids as precursors for the preparation of heteroatom-doped CDs (Li et al., 2016). Wang et al. synthesized N-doped carbon dots using 1-carboxyethyl-3-methyl imidazole chloride ionic liquid as the carbon source, and the obtained CDs were applied as excellent label-free multifunctional fluorescent probes for Hg2+, Cu2+, and S2O3 2− with detection limits of 0.076, 0.125, and 1.17 μmol L−1, respectively (Wang et al., 2019b). In another study, CDs were prepared via a microwave-assisted method using 1-vinyl-3-aminopropyl imidazolium ionic liquid and ethylenediamine as precursors. These CDs displayed excellent selectivity for Cr6+, with a detection limit of 0.015 μmol L−1 (Liu et al., 2017a). Although numerous studies have applied CDs derived from ionic liquids as fluorescent probes, their use for the detection of sulfathiazole antibiotics has not yet been reported.

1-Allyl-3-vinylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([AVIm][BF4]) is an imidazolium-based ionic liquid containing the BF4 − anion and it can be used as the ideal carbon source for the preparation of N, B, F co-doped CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 , a novel fluorescent probe based on N, B, F-CDs was prepared via a facile hydrothermal approach using malonate as the carbon source and [AVIm][BF4] as a carbon source and a doping agent. The fluorescence of the probe was significantly quenched in the presence of STZ due to IFE. The critical parameters, namely the concentration of N, B, F-CDs, incubation time, and pH, affecting the fluorescence of the probe were optimized, and the probe was successfully applied to detect STZ in river water, soil, milk, and egg samples.

1-Allyl-3-vinylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate (99%, CAS: 936030-54-5) was procured from Shanghai Chengjie Chemical Co. Ltd. (China). Analytical grade sulfathiazole (98%, CAS: 72-14-0), sulfapyridine (98%, CAS: 144-83-2), metronidazole (99%, CAS: 443-48-1), sulfamethoxazole (98%, CAS: 723-46-6), sulfadiazine (98%, CAS: 68-35-9), sulfamonomethoxine (93%, CAS: 1220-83-3), sulfamethazine (99%, CAS: 57-68-1), and malonate (99.5%, CAS: 141-82-2) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Industrial Co. Ltd. (China). Pure water for all experiments was supplied by Wahaha Co. Ltd. (China).

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer (California, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a Perkin-Elmer 983 infrared spectrometer (Norwalk, USA). The morphologies of the samples were gained on a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Hillsboro, USA). UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Pgeneral T6 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing, China). The fluorescence lifetime experiments were conducted using an Edinburgh FLS 980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, British). All centrifugation steps were performed using a Hitachi CF16RN high-speed centrifuge (Tokyo, Japan).

The proposed N, B, F-CDs were prepared by following a simple hydrothermal synthetic approach. Equimolar amounts of [AVIm][BF4] (1.6650 g, 0.0075 mol) and malonate (0.7805 g, 0.0075 mol) were added to a polytetrafluoroethylene reaction kettle containing 20 mL pure water, and the reaction mixture was stirred until a clear solution was formed. The reaction kettle and its contents were then heated in an oven at 220 °C for 12 h. The reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature and the resulting brown aqueous solution containing the N, B, F-CDs was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove the impurities. The filtrate was collected in a brown bottle and stored at 4 °C for use in subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of STZ and interference experiments
ce:section.ce:para: 80 μL of N, B, F-CDs solution and various concentrations of STZ were successively added into a centrifuge tube. The resulting solutions were diluted with water such that the total volume after dilution was 2 mL. The solutions were incubated at ambient temperature for 2 min and then the fluorescence intensity was evaluated at an excitation wavelength of 284 nm with a slit of 5 nm.

The interference experiments were performed with the structural analogs such as sulfadiazine (SDZ), sulfapyridine (SPD), sulfamethoxazole (SMO), sulfamonomethoxine (SMM), metronidazole (MD) and sulfamethazine (SMZ) to examine the selectivity of the fluorescence probe for STZ.

Four real samples including river water, soil, milk and egg were selected to assess the detection performance of the fluorescent probe. The river water and soil samples were obtained from the Wei River and a farm in Xinxiang, respectively. Milk and egg samples were randomly purchased from the local market. Before the detection study, all samples were pretreated. Pretreatment of the water sample was conducted by filtering it through a 0.45 μm filter to remove impurities. The soil sample was air-dried, ground, and then passed through a 100-mesh screen. This was followed by extracting 5 g the sample with 100 mL acetonitrile for 0.5 h. The resulting solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min to separate the sediment. The supernatant was evaporated to near dryness in a rotary evaporator and then dissolved in water. Milk and egg samples (5 g) were extracted with 100 mL acidified acetonitrile (0.1% acetic acid) for 0.5 h to remove lipids and proteins. The solution was centrifuged and the obtained supernatants were evaporated to near dryness and then dissolved in water. All the obtained pretreated samples were collected and analyzed by the probe at the blank and spiked concentration (50, 100, 150 ng mL−1). The spiked samples were prepared by adding the required amount of STZ into the pretreated samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of N, B, F-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM and FTIR spectra of the obtained N, B, F-CDs are shown in supplementary material section 1 (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. S1E, the UV–vis spectrum of the synthesized N, B, F-CDs displayed a strong absorption peak at 225 nm corresponding to the π-π* transition of the aromatic CC bonds, which is considered to be the reason for the enhanced fluorescence displayed by N, B, F-CDs (Liu et al., 2017b). The maximum fluorescence emission of the prepared N, B, F-CDs was obtained at 385 nm with an excitation wavelength of 284 nm. Interestingly, modulating the excitation wavelength from 230 to 420 nm caused a shift in the emission wavelength, indicating that the N, B, F-CDs showed an excitation-dependent fluorescence property (Fig. S1F). This property can arise due to the existing miscellaneous energy traps on the surface of the N, B, F-CDs (Liu et al., 2016).

Additionally, the effect of pH on the fluorescence stability of the prepared N, B, F-CDs was studied (Fig. S2A). It was observed that the fluorescence intensity increased with increasing pH in the range of 1.0–3.0 but remained stable within the pH range of 3.0–10.0. Maximum fluorescence was observed at pH 7.0 and the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly when the pH values were greater than 10.0. This indicated that the N, B, F-CDs exhibited excellent optical stability between pH 3.0 and 10.0. The influence of ion strength on the fluorescent properties of N, B, F-CDs was also investigated (Fig. S2B). Herein, sodium and chloride ions were chosen as the coexisting ions. Different concentrations of NaCl solution (0–4.5 mol L−1) were added to 80 μL of N, B, F-CD solution, and the fluorescence spectra of the resulting solutions were recorded at 385 nm. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of the N, B, F-CDs decreased with an increase in the ionic strength of the solution, suggesting that the presence of ions caused the CDs to agglomerate. This also indicated that the N, B, F-CDs are highly sensitive toward ions. The fluorescence stability of N, B, F-CDs was also examined by measuring the fluorescence intensity of the solution after one month (Fig. S2C). It was found that the fluorescence intensity remained unchanged, indicating that the synthesized N, B, F-CDs possessed good fluorescence stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the detection of STZ
ce:section.ce:para: As seen in Fig. 2A, the presence of STZ had a significant impact on the fluorescence intensity of N, B, F-CDs and the addition of STZ caused up to 60.7% quenching of the fluorescence emitted by the probe. Fluorescence quenching can occur via various mechanisms, such as IFE, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), electron transfer, static quenching effect and dynamic quenching effect (Zu et al., 2017). Analysis of the UV–vis absorption spectra and the fluorescence lifetimes provided insight into the mechanism of the STZ-induced fluorescence quenching demonstrated by the probe. The UV–vis spectrum of N, B, F-CDs/STZ was simply a physical superposition of the individual N, B, F-CD and STZ spectra (Fig. 2B). Two characteristic peaks could be identified. The peak located at 285 nm was attributed to the strong characteristic absorption of STZ and another peak at 225 nm corresponded to the characteristic absorption of the N, B, F-CDs. Interestingly, the addition of STZ did not cause a shift in the characteristic peak (225 nm) of the N, B, F CDs. Moreover, new peaks were also not observed. These findings indicated that there was no chemical reaction or electron transfer between the STZ and the fluorescent N, B, F-CDs, and excluded static quenching that could make the UV–vis spectrum change due to the formation of the ground-state complex (Han et al., 2019a; Zu et al., 2017). The spectrum for STZ showed a strong UV absorption peak at 240–320 nm, which overlaps well with the fluorescence excitation spectrum of N, B, F-CDs (Fig. 2C), implying that the excitation light of N, B, F-CDs can be absorbed by STZ, resulting in a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of N, B, F-CDs. Based on these results, fluorescence quenching may occur via IFE or FRET (Lin et al., 2016; Sun and Lei, 2017).

Fluorescence lifetimes were also evaluated to verify the mechanism of fluorescence quenching induced by STZ (Fig. 2D). It was found that the presence of STZ did not affect the fluorescence lifetimes of N, B, F-CDs. The average fluorescence lifetimes of N, B, F-CDs, in the presence and absence of STZ, were almost equal, 13.862 ns and 13.869 ns, respectively. This indicates that FRET process does not occur in the detection system because FRET significantly changes the fluorescence lifetimes (Chen et al., 2018; Hu and Gao, 2020). These results verified that the detection mechanism of the presented probe was ascribed to the IFE between the N, B, F-CDs and STZ (Qian et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of N, B, F-CDs concentration
ce:section.ce:para: As a key parameter, the amount of N, B, F-CDs needs to be considered while developing a fluorescent probe. Lower concentrations of N, B, F-CDs can enhance the response of the probe toward STZ and display higher sensitivity while higher concentrations provide a broader detection range but display a weaker response toward STZ. Therefore, the amount of N, B, F-CDs required to obtain the best detection results was optimized. Fluorescence intensities of solutions containing 3 μg mL−1 STZ and different amounts of N, B, F-CDs were measured according to the method described in section 2.4. It was observed that the fluorescence response value, (F0 F)/F, significantly increased as the concentration of N, B, F-CDs increased from 15 to 40 μL mL−1 (Fig. S3A). The maximum fluorescence response was recorded at 40 μL mL−1 N, B, F-CDs concentration, and it decreased gradually with further increase in the N, B, F-CDs concentration, suggesting that the fluorescent probe possessed the highest sensitivity at 40 μL mL−1 concentration. Therefore, subsequent experiments were performed with 40 μL mL−1 of N, B, F-CDs concentration.

As mentioned previously, the fluorescence intensity of N, B, F-CDs remained unaffected within the pH range of 3.0 to 10.0. However, the effect of pH in the presence of STZ needs to be confirmed. Therefore, the detection experiments were performed at different pH values (Fig. S3B). It was observed that the fluorescence response value of the probe was also maintained in the pH range of 3.0 to 10.0, suggesting the stability of STZ detection in this pH range. These results demonstrate that the N, B, F-CDs possess excellent fluorescence stability and, thus, can be used for the detection of STZ in complex samples. Subsequent detection experiments were performed at pH = 7.0, as most practical samples were neutral solutions.

Reaction time can significantly impact the performance of the probe, which needs to be considered for the application of the fluorescent probe. Hence, the reaction time of the fluorescent probe to detect STZ was investigated. As shown in Fig. S3C, the fluorescence of N, B, F-CDs was quenched rapidly after the addition of STZ. The maximum fluorescence response value was attained in two minutes and that almost remained unchanged with the continuous increase of time, indicating that the detection reaction can reach equilibrium within two minutes. Therefore, the optimal reaction time was found to be 2 min for this detection system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of N, B, F-CDs probe for STZ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the sensitivity of the probe under the optimum conditions, the fluorescence intensity of the probe was measured after the addition of different amounts of STZ (0–50 μg mL−1). As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence intensity of N, B, F-CDs at 385 nm gradually decreased as STZ concentration increased from 0 to 50 μg mL−1. Therefore, the fluorescence response of N, B, F-CDs linearly varied with the STZ concentrations in two wide concentration levels (Fig. 3B, C, and D), which ranged from 0.008 to 10 μg mL−1 and 10 to 45 μg mL−1 with high correlation coefficients of 0.9998 and 0.9992, respectively. The detection limit for STZ was as low as 5.5 ng mL−1 based on 3σ/s (σ is the standard deviation of blank signal (n = 20), and s is the slope of the calibration curve). More importantly, the proposed fluorescent probe exhibited higher sensitivity and a wider linear range than those shown by other existing methods for STZ detection (Table 1 ) (An et al., 2018; Dai et al., 2017; Gokoglu and Yilmaz, 2014; Zazouli et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2018). Although HPLC and HPLC-MS/MS displayed considerable sensitivity, they suffered from various limitations, such as the requirement of complex instruments, tedious preparation of MIP-SPE, and enrichment and elution process of the sample. By contrast, the proposed fluorescent probe is a convenient method because of its simple synthesis, cheap equipment and rapid measurement. Therefore, the developed N, B, F-CDs can be an excellent and competitive probe for the determination of trace STZ.

To assess the detection performance and accuracy of this method, precision and stability assays of the synthesized fluorescence probe were performed. Three concentrations of STZ (0.025, 0.10, and 0.25 μg mL−1) were used for the intra-and inter-day precision experiments. For the intra-day precision study, the fluorescence response of the probe was detected 6 times a day, while for inter-day precision study, the fluorescence responses were detected once a day for 6 days. The result in Table S1 showed that the quantification of the intra-day and the inter-day precisions were in good agreement with three declared concentrations of 0.025, 0.10, and 0.25 μg mL−1, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were 3.7%, 1.1%, 0.8% (intra-day) and 4.8%, 1.9%, 1.0% (inter-day), respectively. All RSD values are less than 5%, which confirms the reproducibility of this method. Additionally, to evaluate the stability of the fluorescence probe, we continuously recorded the fluorescence response of the probe at 0.025, 0.10 and 0.25 μg mL−1 concentration of STZ within 24 h, respectively (Fig. S4). The analysis showed that there were no significant changes in the fluorescence responses of the probe at all three concentrations after 24 h. These results confirmed that the proposed method possesses high precision and stability.

Since selectivity of a fluorescent probe is critical for its application in complicated real samples, the effect of STZ structural analogs (Fig. S5) on the selectivity performance of the fluorescence probe was investigated. Firstly, a certain amount of 0.1 μg mL−1 STZ, 0.5 μg mL−1 SPD, MD, and SMO, 0.3 μg mL−1 SDZ, SMM, and SMZ solutions were added to 40 μL mL−1 N, B, F-CDs solution, respectively. The fluorescence responses of the fluorescence probe toward STZ and its structural analogs were recorded and are shown in Fig. 4 (Single). The results clearly demonstrate that the fluorescence quenching effect of the six structural analogs toward the N, B, F-CDs is negligible in comparison with the fluorescence quenching induced by STZ. Furthermore, the influence of the solution containing STZ and its structural analogs on the fluorescence properties of N, B, F-CDs was also investigated. It can be seen from Fig. 4 (Mixture) that the fluorescent quenching effect induced by STZ was not significantly disturbed in the presence of its structural analogs. This indicates that the prepared fluorescent probe had high selectivity and specificity in the complex system, which suggests its potential application for the rapid identification and detection of STZ in real samples with complex matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of STZ in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability and practicability of the fluorescent probe in complicated actual environmental samples was further evaluated. The fluorescent probe was used for the determination of STZ in blank and spiked samples of river water, soil, milk and egg, as listed in Table 2 . No STZ was detected in any of the blank samples, but the STZ could be identified well in all spiked samples. Good spiked recoveries of STZ were achieved ranged from 96.1 to 101.6% with RSDs in the range of 0.6–4.4% (n = 6). The results reveal that the developed novel fluorescent probe based on the N, B, F-CDs can detect trace amounts of STZ with high sensitivity and accuracy in complicated real samples. This verifies the practicability of the probe for the detecting trace STZ in the environment.

In summary, a novel fluorescent probe based on N, B, F-CDs was prepared via a simple one-step hydrothermal method using malonate and 1-allyl-3-vinylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate as the precursors. Based on the IFE between N, B, F-CDs and STZ, the developed N, B, F-CDs could be successfully employed as a highly sensitive novel fluorescent probe for the rapid detection of STZ. Under optimal conditions, the N, B, F-CDs-based probe exhibited two wide linear ranges of 0.008 to 10 μg mL−1 and 10 to 45 μg mL−1, and provided a low detection limit of 5.5 ng mL−1 for the determination of STZ. Moreover, the excellent fluorescence probe was successfully applied for the detection of trace STZ from complex practical samples and good spiked recoveries of STZ in the range of 96.1–101.6% were obtained. The developed method possesses the following advantages: (1) the N, B, F-CDs with good optical and chemical stabilities were synthesized through a facile and simple procedure; (2) this sensing system exhibits low cost, excellent selectivity, high sensitivity and environmental friendliness; (3) the proposed strategy possesses good potential for detection of STZ in environmental samples. As most previous reports, the limitation of STZ determination method is that the sample pretreatment is still needed when analyzing the actual samples. It's expected that the proposed fluorescent probe can be combined with the ratiometric fluorescence technology to construct fluorescent paper strips for realizing on-site real sample determination in the future. The developed method provides a promising platform for environmental sample monitoring and food analysis in environmental protection and food safety fields, and it also shows the potential application in the field related to biomedicine, such as biomedicine diagnosis.

Letian Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Yongli Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing - original draft, Project administration. Guohao Cheng: Writing - review & editing. Zegang Fan: Investigation, Data curation. Jinyu Yuan: Investigation, Software. Shuailong He: Data curation, Software. Guifen Zhu: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Formal analysis, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21876045 and 21507023), Program for Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology in the University of Henan Province (No. 20IRTSTHN011), Key Scientific Research Project Plan of Henan Province (No. 20A610007), and Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars of Henan Normal University (No. 2020JQ05).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2020.143432.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: An et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of sulfonamides in milk by capillary electrophoresis with PEG@MoS2 as a dispersive solid-phase extraction sorbent
R. Soc. Open Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: An, J., Wang, X., Ming, M., Li, J., Ye, N., 2018. Determination of sulfonamides in milk by capillary electrophoresis with PEG@MoS2 as a dispersive solid-phase extraction sorbent. R. Soc. Open Sci. 5, 172104-172114.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Ashrafizadeh et al., 2020
